title: Schedule C for Gig Workers: Complete Walkthrough for 2026 Taxes
metaTitle: Schedule C for Gig Workers: Full 2026 Tax Walkthrough
metaDescription: Step-by-step Schedule C walkthrough for Uber, DoorDash, Instacart, and other gig workers filing 2025 taxes in 2026. Line by line, plain English.
excerpt: If you drove for Uber, delivered for DoorDash, or shopped for Instacart in 2025, you owe Schedule C with your 1040. Here is how to fill it out without losing your mind.
body:
If you earned even one dollar from gig work in 2025, the IRS expects a Schedule C tucked into your 1040 this filing season. Schedule C is where you report self-employment income and write off business expenses, and for most gig workers it is the single most important form of the year. Get it right and your tax bill drops significantly. Get it wrong and you either overpay or invite an audit letter.

This walkthrough goes line by line through the 2025 Schedule C (the form you file in 2026), with examples geared toward rideshare drivers, delivery couriers, and other 1099 gig workers.

Who Needs to File Schedule C

You file Schedule C if you operated a business or worked as a sole proprietor during the year. For gig workers, that means anyone who:

  • Drove for Uber, Lyft, or any rideshare app
  • Delivered for DoorDash, Uber Eats, Grubhub, Instacart, Shipt, Amazon Flex, or similar
  • Did Tasks on TaskRabbit, walked dogs on Rover, or freelanced on Fiverr or Upwork
  • Earned $400 or more in net self-employment income (the threshold that triggers SE tax)

Even if you only earned $300 from a side hustle, you should still file Schedule C to report the income. The $400 number is just where self-employment tax kicks in.

Part I: Income

The top of Schedule C asks for your business name, address, principal business code (rideshare and delivery drivers use 485300 or 492000), and accounting method (cash, for almost everyone).

Line 1: Gross Receipts or Sales

Add up every dollar the platforms paid you. This is your gross earnings, not your take-home. Include tips, bonuses, referral payments, and incentives. Your 1099-NEC from each platform shows part of this. Your 1099-K (issued if you cleared the threshold) shows passenger payments processed through the app.

Important: if you drove for both Uber and Lyft, you combine the totals. One Schedule C covers all your gig driving as one trade or business. You only need separate Schedule Cs if you ran genuinely different businesses (driving plus a wedding photography side hustle, for example).

Line 2-7: Returns, Cost of Goods Sold, Gross Profit

Most gig workers leave Lines 2 through 6 blank. You did not return anything and you did not sell physical inventory. Line 7 (gross income) just copies Line 1.

Part II: Expenses

This is where the real money is. Every legitimate business expense lowers your taxable income and your self-employment tax.

Line 9: Car and Truck Expenses

For most gig drivers, this is the biggest deduction by far. You pick one of two methods (and you generally cannot switch back and forth):

  • Standard mileage rate. For 2025 returns, the IRS rate is 70 cents per business mile. The 2026 rate has not been published yet and is expected by late 2025. Multiply business miles by the rate and enter the total.
  • Actual expense method. Add up gas, oil, repairs, insurance, depreciation, lease payments, and registration, then multiply by your business use percentage.

You also fill out Part IV of Schedule C with vehicle details: date placed in service, total miles, business miles, commuting miles, and personal miles. If you used standard mileage, you do not need to itemize the actual costs.

Line 10-27: Other Expenses

Common gig worker deductions:

  • Line 15 Insurance. Rideshare endorsement on your auto policy (the business portion only).
  • Line 17 Legal and professional services. Your tax prep fee for the business portion.
  • Line 18 Office expense. Phone mounts, dash cams, chargers.
  • Line 22 Supplies. Hot bags, water bottles for passengers, cleaning supplies for the car.
  • Line 25 Utilities. Business-use percentage of your cell phone bill.
  • Line 27a Other expenses. Platform commissions and fees (if not already netted out), tolls, parking, car washes, AAA membership.

You can list additional expenses on a Part V continuation sheet at the bottom of the form.

Line 28-31: Net Profit or Loss

Add all expenses (Line 28), subtract from gross income (Line 29 after home office, if any), and you get net profit or loss on Line 31. This number flows to Schedule SE (self-employment tax) and to Line 8 of your Schedule 1, which feeds your 1040.

Part III: Cost of Goods Sold

Skip this. Gig drivers and couriers do not sell physical goods.

Part IV: Vehicle Information

If you claimed car expenses, fill out:

  • Date the vehicle was placed in service for business (the first day you drove for a platform)
  • Total miles driven during the year (odometer end minus odometer start)
  • Business miles
  • Commuting miles (usually zero for gig drivers, since your first pickup is your first business stop)
  • Personal miles (the leftover)
  • Whether the vehicle is available for personal use, whether you have another vehicle, and whether you have written records

Your tracking app or logbook backs up these numbers. See our list of recommended mileage tracking apps if you do not have one yet.

Part V: Other Expenses

Any expense that did not fit a Part II category goes here with a brief description. Be specific: "Platform service fees" or "Hot bag and delivery supplies" reads better than "misc."

Common Mistakes to Avoid

  • Forgetting tips and bonuses. Cash tips count even if no 1099 shows them.
  • Double-counting platform fees. If Uber already subtracted commission before sending you a 1099-K, do not deduct it again.
  • Claiming commuting miles. The miles from your home to the first pickup are still business miles for most gig drivers (the IRS treats your home as the business base), but check the latest guidance.
  • Mixing personal and business. Only the business portion of insurance, phone, and other shared costs is deductible.

What You Need Before You Start

DocumentWhere to Get It
1099-NEC from each platformTax tab in driver app, usually available by January 31
1099-K from each platformSame place, if you hit the reporting threshold
Mileage logYour tracking app export
Expense receiptsFolder, email, or expense app
Bank statementsFor verifying deposits
Prior year Schedule CFor comparison and rollover items
Vehicle recordsPurchase docs, registration, insurance declarations

Our free tools page includes a Schedule C estimator that does the math for you before you sit down with software or a CPA.

The 1099 Threshold Trap

Platforms only issue 1099-NEC forms if your earnings cleared $600 in a calendar year. If you earned $550 from a single platform, you will not get a 1099, but you still owe tax on every dollar. The IRS sees platform deposits through bank reporting, so do not assume that no 1099 means no reporting. Add up every dollar you received and report the full amount on Schedule C.

The 1099-K threshold (for third-party payment processors and rideshare passenger fares) has been moving around. Whatever the current threshold, the income is taxable whether or not the form arrives. Pull your annual summary from each platform's tax center to verify your numbers.

How Schedule C Connects to the Rest of Your Return

Schedule C does not stand alone. Its net profit number drives three other parts of your return:

  • Schedule SE calculates self-employment tax on the net profit (15.3% of 92.35% of net earnings)
  • Schedule 1 Line 3 is where Schedule C net profit lands as business income
  • Schedule 1 Line 15 picks up half of your SE tax as a deduction
  • Form 8995 or 8995-A may give you a 20% Qualified Business Income deduction on your Schedule C profit, depending on your total income

Our SE tax explainer walks through how Schedule SE handles the 15.3% calculation.

When You File and What You Pay

Schedule C is due with your Form 1040, generally April 15, 2026 for 2025 returns. If you owe more than $1,000, the IRS expects quarterly estimated payments throughout the year. Our quarterly tax guide walks through how to calculate and send those.

Filing Software vs. CPA vs. DIY Paper

You have three reasonable paths to filing Schedule C:

  • Tax software. TurboTax Self-Employed, H&R Block Premium, FreeTaxUSA, and TaxAct all handle Schedule C. Pricing ranges from free (FreeTaxUSA) to $130-plus (TurboTax). Most gig workers do fine with software once they have their numbers organized.
  • CPA or enrolled agent. Expect $300 to $700 for a simple Schedule C return. Worth it the first year if your income is significant or if you have unusual items (depreciation, S-corp considerations, multiple state filings).
  • Paper filing. Free but slow. The IRS encourages e-filing, and refunds via paper file can take three months instead of three weeks.

Whatever path you pick, do not wait until April 14. Self-employed returns take longer than W-2 returns, and rushed prep is where errors happen.

If your refund is smaller than expected or you suddenly owe a balance you did not budget for, a short-term cash advance can bridge the gap until your next earnings cycle. PennyLime funds advances of $500 to $10,000 within 24 to 48 hours with no credit check, repaid through weekly ACH debits.

Bottom Line

Schedule C looks intimidating the first year. After one full pass, the form becomes a familiar checklist. Track your miles, keep your receipts, separate personal and business as much as possible, and the numbers fall into place. If you need help bridging tax-season cash flow while you wait for your refund or sort out a balance due, apply for a PennyLime cash advance and get an answer the same day.

title: Mileage Deduction vs Actual Expense Method: Best Choice for Gig Drivers
metaTitle: Mileage vs Actual Expense: Best Method for Gig Drivers
metaDescription: Standard mileage or actual expense method? A complete side-by-side comparison for Uber, Lyft, DoorDash, and Instacart drivers with worked examples for 2026.
excerpt: The IRS lets gig drivers pick between two ways to deduct vehicle costs. One is easier. One can be larger. Here is how to figure out which one saves you more.
body:
Every gig driver faces the same choice on Schedule C: use the standard mileage rate or the actual expense method. Pick the right one and you can save hundreds or even thousands of dollars. Pick the wrong one and you leave money on the table or lock yourself out of the better option for years.

This article breaks down how each method works, runs the math on real driver scenarios, and gives you a simple rule of thumb to decide.

How the Standard Mileage Method Works

You count every business mile you drove and multiply by the IRS rate. For 2025 returns (filing in 2026), the business standard mileage rate is 70 cents per mile. The 2026 rate is typically announced by the IRS in late 2025.

Example: you drove 18,000 business miles in 2025. Your deduction is 18,000 times 0.70, which equals $12,600. That number goes on Schedule C Line 9.

What is included in that 70 cents:

  • Gasoline and oil
  • Routine maintenance and repairs
  • Tires
  • Insurance
  • Registration fees
  • Depreciation or lease payments

What is NOT included (you can still deduct these separately):

  • Tolls and parking fees on business trips
  • Loan interest (business portion)
  • Personal property taxes on the vehicle (business portion)

How the Actual Expense Method Works

You track every penny you spent on the car for the year, then multiply the total by your business use percentage.

Categories you total up:

  • Gas and oil
  • Repairs and maintenance
  • Tires
  • Insurance (including the rideshare endorsement)
  • Registration and license
  • Lease payments or depreciation (purchased vehicles)
  • Car washes

Business use percentage is business miles divided by total miles. If you drove 18,000 business miles out of 25,000 total miles, your business use is 72%.

Example: total annual car costs of $15,000, business use 72%. Deduction equals $15,000 times 0.72, which is $10,800.

Side-by-Side Comparison

FactorStandard MileageActual Expense
RecordkeepingJust mileage logMileage plus every receipt
Best forFuel-efficient older carsExpensive cars, lots of repairs
DepreciationBuilt into the rateCalculated separately
Switch flexibilityCan switch to actual laterLocked in for the vehicle's life if you start here on a purchased car
Audit riskLower (simpler)Higher (more line items to defend)

Three Realistic Examples

Example 1: Toyota Prius, 22,000 business miles

Standard mileage: 22,000 times $0.70 equals $15,400.

Actual: $1,800 gas, $600 insurance share, $400 maintenance, $300 depreciation share, totaling roughly $3,100. Standard mileage wins by a wide margin.

Verdict: standard mileage by $12,000+. The Prius sips gas, so actual expenses stay low while mileage piles up.

Example 2: Ford F-150, 16,000 business miles

Standard mileage: 16,000 times $0.70 equals $11,200.

Actual: $4,200 gas, $1,400 insurance share, $1,800 maintenance, $3,000 depreciation share, totaling roughly $10,400. Standard mileage still wins, but barely.

Verdict: standard mileage by $800. Heavy trucks burn more fuel and need more maintenance, but the standard rate is generous.

Example 3: Used Mercedes E-Class, 14,000 business miles, $5,000 transmission repair

Standard mileage: 14,000 times $0.70 equals $9,800.

Actual: $2,800 gas, $1,800 insurance share, $5,400 repairs (including the transmission), $1,200 depreciation share, totaling roughly $11,200. Actual wins.

Verdict: actual by $1,400. Big repair years and luxury vehicles tip the math toward actual.

The Switching Rule Most Drivers Miss

The IRS has a one-way door on this choice for purchased vehicles. If you use the actual expense method in the FIRST year you place the car in service for business, you must keep using actual expense for that vehicle for as long as you own it.

If you use standard mileage in the first year, you can switch to actual later. You just have to use straight-line depreciation going forward.

Leased vehicles are even stricter. Whichever method you pick in year one is locked in for the entire lease.

Practical takeaway: in year one, leaning toward standard mileage keeps your options open.

What Counts as a Business Mile

This is the same regardless of method, and it is where many drivers shortchange themselves.

  • Mile from home to your first ping or pickup: business
  • Miles between trips while app is on: business
  • Miles during a trip with passenger or order: business
  • Miles repositioning to a high-demand zone: business
  • Miles back home after your last drop: business
  • Miles to the gas station, car wash, or mechanic during work: business
  • Miles to pick up groceries for yourself: personal

If you do not have a tracking app yet, our roundup of best mileage apps for gig drivers covers the top options.

A Simple Decision Framework

Use standard mileage if:

  • Your car gets decent fuel economy
  • You drive a lot of miles (over 15,000 business miles per year)
  • You hate paperwork
  • This is the first year you placed the car in service for business

Use actual expense if:

  • You drive a fuel-thirsty truck or SUV
  • You had a major repair year
  • Your business use percentage is high (over 80%)
  • You bought a heavy vehicle that qualifies for Section 179 or bonus depreciation

Recordkeeping Either Way

Both methods require you to track total miles, business miles, and the date the vehicle was placed in service. Both go on Part IV of Schedule C. Both want a contemporaneous log (recorded close to when the trip happened), not a guess you write in April.

Apps like MileIQ, Stride, Gridwise, and Everlance auto-track and let you swipe trips as business or personal. Pair that with our full list of gig worker deductions and you have your Schedule C inputs ready before tax day.

What Counts in Actual Expense Math

If you go the actual expense route, here is the full menu of what you can include:

  • Gas and oil. Every fill-up, full year.
  • Repairs and maintenance. Oil changes, brakes, tires, alignments, transmission work, anything that keeps the car running.
  • Insurance. Full premium including the rideshare endorsement.
  • Registration and license. Annual fees and renewal costs.
  • Lease payments. The full monthly cost (subject to a lease inclusion amount for high-end vehicles).
  • Depreciation. If you own the vehicle, you depreciate it over five years under MACRS, subject to luxury auto caps.
  • Car washes and detailing. Yes, these count.

Add all that up, multiply by business use percentage, and that is your actual expense deduction. Tolls and parking are separately deductible on top of either method.

Hidden Costs of Switching Methods

Beyond the lock-in rule, switching from standard mileage to actual triggers a basis adjustment. The IRS treats prior standard mileage as having included a depreciation component (about 30 cents per mile in 2024, slightly less for 2025). When you switch, you reduce the vehicle's basis by that accumulated depreciation. If you eventually sell the car, that reduction can mean a bigger taxable gain at sale time.

This is why most gig drivers should run the math both ways in their head every year but only formally switch when the savings are large and durable.

What If You Bought a Brand New EV

Electric vehicles complicate the calculation. Gas costs disappear, but electricity costs (and a portion of your home utility bill if you charge at home) become deductible under actual expense. Depreciation on a $50,000 EV under luxury auto caps spreads slowly. The standard mileage rate is unchanged whether you drive a gas car or an EV, which often makes standard mileage the better choice for EV drivers despite the lower fuel costs.

The federal EV tax credit (separate from Schedule C) may also apply on the personal portion of the purchase, depending on income and vehicle eligibility.

When the Math Gets Tight

If your two calculations are within a few hundred dollars of each other, lean toward standard mileage. The audit risk is lower, the prep time is shorter, and you preserve your right to switch to actual in a future year if circumstances change.

Multi-Vehicle Households

If you swap between two vehicles for gig work, each vehicle gets its own election. You can use standard mileage on one and actual on another, but each vehicle's method is locked in by its first-year choice. Track miles separately for each vehicle on its own Part IV.

Most drivers keep things simple by using a single dedicated vehicle for gig work. The deduction is cleaner, the audit trail is clearer, and your insurance is simpler to manage.

Spousal Vehicles

If your spouse owns the vehicle and you drive it for gig work, you can still deduct vehicle expenses on Schedule C as long as you bear the cost of operation (you pay for the gas, insurance, repairs from your accounts). The IRS focuses on economic reality, not legal title.

That said, splitting business use between two filers in the same household requires careful tracking. Keep records that show who paid for what and who drove which miles.

Cash flow during tax season is its own challenge. If you find yourself owing more than expected and your next big earnings week is a few days out, a cash advance from PennyLime can cover the IRS or extend your runway. Advances of $500 to $10,000, no credit check, funded in 24 to 48 hours.

Bottom Line

Run both numbers once a year. For 80% of gig drivers, standard mileage wins, especially with the 2025 rate at 70 cents per mile. If you have a heavy vehicle, low total mileage, or a brutal repair year, do the actual expense math. And if you need to bridge tax-season cash flow, apply for a PennyLime cash advance in minutes.

title: Best Mileage Tracking Apps for Gig Drivers in 2026
metaTitle: Best Mileage Tracking Apps for Gig Drivers in 2026
metaDescription: Honest reviews of MileIQ, Stride, Gridwise, Everlance, Hurdlr, Triplog, and QuickBooks mileage apps for Uber, DoorDash, and Instacart drivers in 2026.
excerpt: A spreadsheet works. A tracking app works better. Here are the apps gig drivers actually use to capture every business mile and save thousands at tax time.
body:
The IRS standard mileage rate for 2025 returns is 70 cents per mile. If you drove 18,000 business miles and forgot to log 3,000 of them, that is $2,100 of deduction you will never get back. A good mileage tracking app captures every trip automatically so you do not have to remember.

This roundup covers the apps gig drivers actually use, including pricing, what each does well, and where each falls short.

What to Look for in a Mileage App

  • Auto-detect driving. The app should start tracking when you move and stop when you park, no buttons required.
  • Swipe to classify. Each trip becomes business or personal with one swipe.
  • IRS-compliant log export. CSV or PDF with date, start, end, miles, and purpose.
  • Battery efficiency. A tracker that drains your phone by 3 p.m. is useless.
  • Backup and history. If your phone dies, your logs should be in the cloud.
  • Free tier. Many drivers test before buying.

1. MileIQ

The granddaddy of mileage apps. Owned by Microsoft, ubiquitous, simple.

  • Pricing: Free for 40 drives per month. Unlimited is $5.99/month or $59.99/year.
  • Strengths: Rock solid auto-detect. Clean swipe interface. IRS-ready PDF reports. Works on iOS and Android without quirks.
  • Weaknesses: Only tracks mileage. No income, expense, or tax features. Free tier runs out fast (most gig drivers hit 40 trips in a single shift).
  • Best for: Drivers who want one job done well and do not need extra features.

2. Stride

Free forever, built specifically for gig workers.

  • Pricing: Free. Yes, completely.
  • Strengths: Auto-mileage tracking plus expense logging by photo. Tax estimator. Health insurance marketplace built in. No paywall.
  • Weaknesses: Auto-tracking is less polished than MileIQ. Battery use can spike. Reports are functional but less pretty.
  • Best for: Drivers who want zero monthly cost and do not mind a slightly rougher interface.

3. Gridwise

Built by former Uber drivers, focused on rideshare and delivery analytics.

  • Pricing: Free tier with basic tracking. Pro is $9.99/month or $79.99/year.
  • Strengths: Auto-mileage plus earnings tracking across platforms. Shows which platform, day, and zone pays best. Airport queue tracker for rideshare. Expense logging.
  • Weaknesses: Pro tier needed for full mileage history. Some features are gimmicky.
  • Best for: Multi-app drivers who want one dashboard for earnings and miles.

4. Everlance

The corporate-feeling option that scaled into the gig world.

  • Pricing: Free for 30 trips per month. Premium is $12/month or $120/year.
  • Strengths: Auto-tracking, expense tracking, and revenue tracking in one app. Bank connection for automatic expense import. Strong reports for CPAs.
  • Weaknesses: Premium is pricier than competitors. Some drivers report battery drain on older Androids.
  • Best for: Drivers with multiple income streams or who want bank-connected expense capture.

5. Hurdlr

The accountant-built app, very feature-heavy.

  • Pricing: Free for limited tracking. Premium is $10/month or $100/year.
  • Strengths: Real-time tax estimate updated every trip. Quarterly tax reminder. Income tracking from connected platforms. Mileage auto-detect.
  • Weaknesses: Interface is busier. Auto-detect occasionally misses short trips.
  • Best for: Drivers who want to see live tax owed throughout the year, not just at tax time.

6. Triplog

Heavy-duty option for high-mileage drivers and small fleets.

  • Pricing: Free tier limited. Premium is $5.99/month or $59.99/year.
  • Strengths: Multiple auto-detect modes (GPS, plug-in OBD, Bluetooth beacon). Strongest accuracy claims. Detailed reporting.
  • Weaknesses: More setup friction. Interface feels dated.
  • Best for: Drivers running 30,000+ business miles a year who need bulletproof tracking.

7. QuickBooks Self-Employed

Intuit's all-in-one for sole proprietors.

  • Pricing: $20/month standalone, or bundled with TurboTax Self-Employed at $25/month.
  • Strengths: Mileage, income, expenses, and quarterly tax estimates in one place. Direct export to TurboTax. Schedule C estimate live.
  • Weaknesses: Expensive compared to standalone mileage apps. Overkill if you only need mileage.
  • Best for: Drivers who plan to file with TurboTax and want one ecosystem.

Quick Comparison Table

AppFree TierPaid Price/YearBeyond Mileage
MileIQ40 trips/mo$60None
StrideUnlimitedFreeExpenses, tax estimate
GridwiseBasic$80Earnings analytics
Everlance30 trips/mo$120Expenses, bank sync
HurdlrLimited$100Live tax estimate
TriplogLimited$60Hardware accuracy
QuickBooks SENone$240Full accounting

What the IRS Requires in a Log

Regardless of which app you pick, the IRS wants:

  • Date of each trip
  • Starting and ending location (general is fine, exact GPS pins are bonus)
  • Business purpose
  • Miles driven

All seven apps above produce IRS-compliant exports. Save your year-end PDF or CSV with your tax records and keep it for at least three years.

Battery and Privacy Tips

Auto-tracking uses GPS continuously, which can drain your battery 15 to 25% faster. To mitigate:

  • Carry a 10,000 mAh power bank or hardwired car charger
  • Turn off auto-tracking when not driving (some apps have a "work hours" setting)
  • Disable background app refresh for apps you do not use during shifts
  • Use a low-power dashboard mount that keeps the phone cool

Privacy: every app collects location data. Read the privacy policy. MileIQ and QuickBooks (Intuit) are bound by enterprise data policies. Smaller apps vary. If you are sensitive about who sees your location history, check whether the app sells anonymized data to third parties and whether you can opt out.

Common Setup Mistakes

  • Forgetting to grant always-on location permission. Auto-tracking does not work if the app only has location access "while in use." Set it to "always."
  • Not classifying trips daily. Logs of 200 unclassified trips at year-end are a nightmare. Swipe each trip within a day or two.
  • Not exporting backups. If you change phones or uninstall the app, you can lose history. Export a CSV monthly to email or cloud storage.
  • Logging only one platform. Most drivers run multiple platforms. The app should capture every business trip regardless of which app the work came from.

Manual Logging When the App Fails

No app is perfect. Trips get missed when the phone is off, the app crashes, or you forget to grant permissions. Keep a backup paper log or notes app entry for any shift where you suspect missing trips. The IRS accepts a credible reconstructed log if it is reasonable and supported by other evidence (platform earnings statements, gas receipts, bank records).

A simple text note with date, start odometer, end odometer, and a one-line purpose can save a chunk of deduction.

How to Read Your App's Year-End Report

At tax time, you need a few numbers from your app:

  • Total miles driven (Part IV Line 44a of Schedule C)
  • Business miles (Line 44b)
  • Commuting miles (Line 44c, usually zero for gig drivers since home is the business base)
  • Other personal miles (Line 44d)

Most apps generate a year-end summary PDF in late December or early January. Download it and save it with your tax records. If you switch apps mid-year, you need both reports, and you have to combine the totals manually.

Apple Watch and Wearable Integrations

A few apps (Gridwise, Hurdlr, Everlance) have Apple Watch or wearable companions that let you start, pause, and classify trips from your wrist. Useful for drivers who do not want to glance at their phone constantly. Not essential, but a nice quality-of-life feature.

What About OBD-II Plug-Ins

Triplog and a few enterprise tools sell OBD-II dongles that plug into your car's port and track mileage from the vehicle's computer instead of GPS. More accurate, more battery-friendly (the dongle is powered by the car), but $50 to $100 upfront and overkill for most solo gig drivers. Worth considering if you run a small fleet or split a vehicle between multiple drivers.

Free Spreadsheet as a Last Resort

If you absolutely refuse to install another app, a Google Sheet with columns for date, start odometer, end odometer, business miles, and purpose works. You will miss trips and spend hours reconstructing miles at year-end. The deduction will be smaller than what an app captures automatically. But it satisfies the IRS contemporaneous log requirement if filled in promptly.

Drivers who try this method usually switch to an app within a few months once they realize how much time the manual logging eats. Track once, deduct correctly, save hours.

How This Connects to Your Tax Return

Whatever app you use, the output feeds Line 9 of Schedule C if you elect standard mileage, or Part IV regardless of method. If you have not decided which method, our comparison of mileage vs actual expense walks through the math.

And if you need a quick tax estimate before you sit down to file, our tools page has a free Schedule C calculator.

What If You Forgot to Track

If you are reading this in April with no log, you have a few options. Pull your platform earnings statements (Uber and Lyft show online miles), bank statements for gas purchases, and Google Timeline or Apple Maps history. Reconstructing 80% of your miles from these sources is better than zero, but expect a smaller deduction and higher audit risk.

Start tracking from day one of the next year, no matter what.

Bottom Line

If you want free, use Stride. If you want polish and you only need mileage, use MileIQ. If you want everything in one place, use Hurdlr or QuickBooks SE. Whatever you pick, install it before your next shift.

And if a slow earnings week or surprise tax bill is squeezing your budget, apply for a PennyLime cash advance. We fund $500 to $10,000 in 24 to 48 hours via your Plaid-linked bank account, no credit check required.

This is general tax information, not advice. Consult a CPA for your situation. PennyLime is a service of 770 Technology LLC.

title: LLC vs Sole Proprietorship for Gig Workers: Which Should You Pick
metaTitle: LLC vs Sole Proprietorship: What Gig Workers Should Pick
metaDescription: Honest comparison of LLC and sole proprietorship for Uber, DoorDash, and Instacart drivers. Cost, liability, taxes, paperwork. Most do not need one.
excerpt: TikTok says you need an LLC. Your CPA says it depends. Here is the honest answer for rideshare drivers, couriers, and other gig workers, with the actual math.
body:
If you drive for Uber or deliver for DoorDash, you have probably seen at least one social media post telling you to form an LLC "for tax purposes" or "to protect your assets." Most of those posts are wrong, or at least incomplete. The truth is that for the average gig worker, an LLC is unnecessary paperwork and a recurring fee that buys very little.

This article walks through what an LLC actually does, what sole proprietorship gives you by default, and which gig workers genuinely benefit from incorporating.

What You Are by Default

The moment you accept your first delivery or rideshare pickup, the IRS considers you a sole proprietor. You do not file paperwork. You do not pay a fee. You are already in business. Your tax return is a Schedule C attached to your 1040, and your self-employment income flows through to your personal return.

Sole proprietorship is the default. It is free. It is automatic.

What an LLC Adds

An LLC (Limited Liability Company) is a state-level legal entity. Forming one involves:

  • Filing Articles of Organization with your state ($50 to $500 typical filing fee)
  • Getting an EIN from the IRS (free)
  • Often an operating agreement
  • Annual report or franchise fee in most states ($0 to $800/year, California is the high end)
  • Separate business bank account (best practice, not strictly required)

What an LLC gives you:

  • Liability protection. In theory, your personal assets are separated from business liabilities.
  • Credibility. Some clients or partners prefer working with an LLC.
  • Optional S-corp election. Once your net income is high enough, an S-corp election (Form 2553) can save self-employment tax.

What an LLC Does NOT Give You

This is the part that gets misrepresented constantly.

  • An LLC does not lower your income tax. A single-member LLC is taxed by default as a sole proprietor. Same Schedule C. Same SE tax. Same outcome.
  • An LLC does not unlock new deductions. Every deduction available to an LLC is also available to a sole proprietor: mileage, phone, supplies, all of it.
  • An LLC does not automatically protect you from a car accident. If you cause an accident while driving for a platform, your personal driving and your personal auto insurance are in play. The LLC does not shield you from personal negligence.

The Liability Question for Gig Drivers

This is where the LLC pitch usually starts: "protect yourself from a lawsuit." Let's be honest about what that means for a rideshare or delivery driver.

Most lawsuits against gig drivers stem from car accidents. In an accident lawsuit, the plaintiff sues the driver personally (negligence behind the wheel) and the platform (Uber, DoorDash, etc.). The LLC does not insulate you from personal driving liability because YOU were driving, not the LLC.

What actually protects you:

  • Commercial or rideshare-endorsed auto insurance
  • The platform's coverage during active trips
  • A personal umbrella policy ($1M for $200-$400/year, more than most LLCs provide)

For non-driving gig work like Fiverr freelancing, an LLC does provide more meaningful contract liability protection, since the work product (not your personal driving) is the source of risk.

The Tax Question: When an S-Corp Election Helps

This is where LLCs CAN genuinely save money, but only past a certain income level.

By default, all your net self-employment income gets hit with 15.3% SE tax. With an S-corp election (available to LLCs and corporations), you pay yourself a "reasonable salary" subject to payroll taxes, and the remainder comes through as distributions that avoid SE tax.

Example: $80,000 net income.

  • As a sole prop: roughly $11,304 SE tax (on 92.35% of $80k).
  • As an S-corp with $50,000 salary: $7,650 payroll tax on the salary plus zero SE tax on the $30,000 distribution.
  • Rough SE tax savings: $3,600.

But an S-corp also costs:

  • Payroll service: $400 to $1,200/year
  • Separate business return (Form 1120-S): $500 to $1,500 at a CPA
  • State payroll tax registration and filings
  • Bookkeeping

Net savings often disappear until your net income clears $60,000 to $80,000 per year of self-employment income. Below that, the overhead eats the savings.

State Fee Reality Check

StateFiling FeeAnnual Fee
California$70$800 minimum franchise tax
Texas$300$0 (unless revenue over threshold)
Florida$125$138.75 annual report
New York$200Plus publication requirement ($1,000+)
Delaware$90$300 franchise tax
Wyoming$100$60

A California gig worker pays $800/year minimum to keep an LLC alive. If your net income is $30,000, an LLC is a net cost, not a benefit.

Who Should Form an LLC

  • Net self-employment income above roughly $60,000 to $80,000 and ready to handle S-corp paperwork
  • Non-driving gig work with real contract liability (freelance development, design, consulting)
  • Real estate side activity
  • You are building toward a brand or hiring employees
  • You want a business bank account and credit profile for the entity

Who Should Stick with Sole Proprietorship

  • Driving for Uber, Lyft, DoorDash, Instacart, or any rideshare or delivery platform
  • Net income under $50,000
  • You do not want monthly bookkeeping or payroll
  • You already have rideshare-endorsed auto insurance and a personal umbrella policy

Paperwork Comparison

TaskSole PropSingle-Member LLCLLC with S-Corp Election
Setup time0 hours2-4 hours4-8 hours plus CPA call
Annual paperworkSchedule CSchedule C plus state report1120-S, K-1, payroll, Schedule C, state report
Bookkeeping neededYes (light)Yes (moderate)Yes (heavy)
Separate bank accountRecommendedRequired in practiceRequired

What About Asset Protection From Other Lawsuits

An LLC can protect personal assets from BUSINESS contracts. If you signed a delivery contract under the LLC and the LLC is sued for breach, your house is not on the hook (assuming you respected the corporate veil).

It does not protect you from:

  • Personal car accidents
  • Personal injuries you cause
  • Tax debts
  • Personally guaranteed business loans

Common Misconceptions

  • "An LLC saves taxes." Only with an S-corp election, and only past a certain income.
  • "An LLC unlocks deductions." No. Same deductions.
  • "You need an LLC to get a business loan." Sole props get business credit too.
  • "Wyoming or Delaware is best." If you live and work in California, you also need to register your out-of-state LLC in California and pay the $800. Form where you operate.
  • "An LLC keeps my home address private." Most states require a registered agent address that becomes public record. Using a third-party registered agent service can preserve some privacy, but it costs $100 to $300 per year on top of state fees.
  • "My DBA is the same as an LLC." No. A DBA ("doing business as") is just a registered trade name. It gives you no liability protection.

What Happens If You Form an LLC Then Stop Gig Work

The LLC does not dissolve itself. You have to file dissolution paperwork with the state and a final tax return. If you forget, the state keeps charging annual fees and may eventually administratively dissolve the LLC, which can leave loose ends on your credit and tax record. Plan an exit before you plan an entry.

The Single Most Useful Thing Sole Props Should Do

Open a separate business checking account. Funnel all platform deposits into it. Pay every business expense from it. Move profit to your personal account periodically. This single move:

  • Makes Schedule C prep ten times easier
  • Establishes a paper trail in case of audit
  • Helps you see actual cash flow
  • Costs $0 with most online banks

You get 90% of the recordkeeping benefit of an LLC without the filing fees, franchise tax, or state paperwork.

The Insurance Question

If you are worried about lawsuit risk, the highest leverage move is insurance, not entity formation. A $1M personal umbrella policy costs $200 to $400 a year and stacks on top of your auto and home liability limits. Combined with a proper rideshare endorsement on your auto policy, you have meaningful protection at a fraction of the cost and complexity of an LLC.

For non-driving gig work, professional liability or errors and omissions insurance can run $300 to $1,000 a year depending on the field. Again, cheaper and more directly relevant than forming an entity.

When You Outgrow Sole Prop

Signs you might be ready to upgrade to an LLC or LLC with S-corp election:

  • Net self-employment income consistently above $70,000
  • You are hiring contractors or employees
  • You are signing contracts that require liability separation
  • You want to build business credit separate from your personal credit
  • You are exploring a brand identity, trademarks, or selling the business someday

None of these apply to most rideshare or delivery drivers. They do apply to freelancers scaling up, side businesses turning into full-time operations, and small product companies. Re-evaluate every year or two.

The Honest Recommendation

If you drive rideshare or deliver, stay a sole proprietor. Keep your Schedule C, your deduction list, and your quarterly tax payments in order. Buy good insurance.

Revisit the LLC question when your net income clears $60,000 or you start non-driving freelance work where contract liability is real.

Cash Flow While You Decide

State filing fees, CPA consultations, and payroll setup can add up to $1,000+ in your first month of incorporating. If you want to test the waters but cash is tight, a short-term PennyLime cash advance can cover the setup costs while you wait for next week's earnings. $500 to $10,000 funded in 24 to 48 hours, no credit check.

Ready to apply? Start your application in under 5 minutes.

title: Quarterly Estimated Taxes for Gig Workers: Step-by-Step How-To
metaTitle: Quarterly Estimated Taxes for Gig Workers: How-To Guide
metaDescription: How to calculate, pay, and avoid penalties on quarterly estimated taxes as a gig worker. Step-by-step for Uber, DoorDash, and Instacart drivers.
excerpt: If you make money driving or delivering, the IRS expects payments four times a year, not once. Here is exactly how to calculate and send them without overpaying.
body:
The IRS calls it "pay as you go." Translated for gig workers: if you owed more than $1,000 last April, the IRS expects you to send quarterly payments throughout the year instead of one big check at tax time. Skip them and you owe interest and penalties on top of your bill.

This guide walks through the four payment dates, the safe-harbor rule, two ways to calculate what you owe, and how to actually send the money.

Who Has to Pay Quarterly

You owe quarterly estimated taxes if both apply:

  • You expect to owe at least $1,000 in federal tax for the year after subtracting any withholding
  • Your withholding plus credits is less than the lesser of 90% of this year's tax OR 100% of last year's tax (110% if your prior year AGI was over $150,000)

For most gig workers with no W-2 job (and therefore no withholding), this means yes, you owe quarterly payments.

The Four Due Dates

QuarterCovers Income FromDue Date
Q1January 1 to March 31April 15
Q2April 1 to May 31June 15
Q3June 1 to August 31September 15
Q4September 1 to December 31January 15 of next year

Notice that the "quarters" are uneven. Q2 is only two months. Q4 is four months. Mark these dates in your phone calendar with reminders one week ahead.

The Safe Harbor Rule (The Easy Way)

If you paid taxes last year, the simplest path is the safe harbor. Pay 100% of last year's total tax in equal installments across the four quarters (or 110% if last year's AGI was over $150,000). Do that and the IRS will not penalize you, even if your actual income spikes.

Example: last year's total tax was $8,000. Pay $2,000 each quarter. Done.

The safe harbor is great if your income is growing year over year. The downside: if your income drops, you are overpaying and will get a refund instead of keeping cash flow.

The Current-Year Method (More Work, More Accurate)

Better matches your actual cash flow but requires you to forecast.

Step 1: Estimate Your Annual Net Income

Take your gross gig earnings year-to-date, subtract estimated expenses (mileage, phone, supplies, fees), and project for the full year.

Example: Q1 gross is $15,000, expenses are $6,000, net is $9,000. Projected annual net: $36,000.

Step 2: Calculate Self-Employment Tax

Net earnings times 0.9235 times 0.153.

Example: $36,000 times 0.9235 equals $33,246. Times 0.153 equals $5,087 SE tax.

Step 3: Calculate Income Tax

Subtract half of SE tax (the deductible portion) and the standard deduction from net income, then apply your federal bracket.

2025 standard deduction for single filers is $15,000 (the IRS adjusts for inflation; check the current figure when you file).

$36,000 net minus $2,544 (half of SE tax) minus $15,000 standard deduction equals $18,456 taxable income. At the 12% bracket, federal income tax is roughly $2,000.

Step 4: Add Them Together

$5,087 SE tax plus $2,000 income tax equals roughly $7,087 total federal tax.

Step 5: Divide by Four

$7,087 divided by 4 equals roughly $1,772 per quarter.

If your income is uneven (you earn more in summer than winter, for example), you can use the "annualized income installment method" on Form 2210 to pay more in busy quarters and less in slow ones.

Do Not Forget State Taxes

If you live in a state with income tax (most states), you owe state quarterly estimates too. State rates vary from 1% to 13%. Check your state's department of revenue site for forms (often called Form 1040-ES at the state level, with different state-specific names).

How to Actually Send the Payment

Four ways:

IRS Direct Pay (Free, Fastest)

Go to IRS.gov/DirectPay. Select "Estimated Tax" and the current tax year. Enter your bank info. Payment debits the next business day. Save the confirmation number.

EFTPS (Free, Requires Enrollment)

Electronic Federal Tax Payment System. Enroll once at EFTPS.gov, get a PIN by mail in about a week, then schedule payments up to 365 days in advance. Good for set-and-forget.

Debit or Credit Card

IRS-approved processors charge a fee (about 1.85% for credit, $2.20 flat for debit). Only useful if you need the float or want credit card rewards.

Check by Mail

Send a check with Form 1040-ES voucher to the address listed in the form instructions. Slowest, most error-prone.

Saving for Quarterly Payments Throughout the Year

The simplest system: every time you get paid by a platform, transfer 25 to 30% to a separate savings account labeled "taxes." When the quarterly date arrives, the money is already there.

Most gig workers should aim to set aside:

  • 25% if net income will be under $50,000
  • 30% if net income will be $50,000 to $100,000
  • 35% if net income will be over $100,000

Add state percentage on top if applicable.

What Happens If You Skip or Underpay

The IRS charges an underpayment penalty, calculated like interest on the missed amount from the date it was due until the date you paid. Rates change quarterly but currently run around 8% annualized.

Two ways to avoid the penalty:

  • Hit the safe harbor
  • Pay at least 90% of your final tax through withholding or estimates by the year's end

What If You Earned Less Than Expected

You can adjust downward in later quarters. If you overpaid Q1 and Q2, send a smaller (or zero) Q3 payment. The IRS does not refund quarterly overpayments mid-year; it nets out at tax time.

What If You Earned a Lot More

Recalculate and increase your remaining payments. If you started a $50,000-year track and suddenly hit a $100,000 pace, your Q3 and Q4 should reflect the new run rate.

The First-Year Trap

The safe harbor rule requires "last year's tax" to use. If this is your first year of self-employment and you had no tax liability the prior year (or had a tiny W-2 refund), the safe harbor offers no protection. You have to use the current-year method.

This is when most new gig workers get blindsided. They earn $40,000 in their first year, file in April, and discover they owe $7,000 in federal tax plus state tax, plus an underpayment penalty because no quarterly payments were made.

Avoid this by setting aside 25 to 30% of every payment from day one and making at least one estimated payment before the year ends, even if you skip the earlier quarters. A late Q4 payment is better than zero estimates.

Quarterly Math in Real Time

Some apps (Hurdlr, QuickBooks Self-Employed, Stride) give you a live running estimate of quarterly tax owed. Useful for two reasons:

  • You can stop and re-budget if your tax bucket is falling behind
  • You enter quarterly dates knowing exactly what to send, no spreadsheet required

Pair an app estimate with manual review before each payment. Apps make mistakes if a deduction is misclassified or your business use percentage shifts.

Estimated Payments and Refundable Credits

If you qualify for refundable credits (Earned Income Tax Credit, Child Tax Credit, Premium Tax Credit), your final tax liability may be lower than your quarterly estimates suggest. You can either:

  • Pay quarterlies based on tax before credits (safest, gets a refund at year-end), or
  • Pay quarterlies based on tax after estimated credits (better cash flow, slight risk if credits change)

If you have kids or low-to-moderate income, run your numbers through a tax estimator before locking in quarterly amounts. Overpaying gives the IRS a free loan; underpaying triggers penalties.

Recordkeeping for Estimated Payments

Keep:

  • Confirmation numbers from each payment
  • Bank statements showing the debit
  • A simple spreadsheet with date, amount, quarter, and method

You will need these on your 1040 next April. Schedule 3 Line 8 (estimated tax payments) is where they go.

How This Fits With Your Whole Tax Picture

Quarterly payments are estimates. Your final liability is reconciled when you file your 1040 with Schedule C and Schedule SE. If you overpaid, you get a refund. If you underpaid, you cut a check.

For the full deduction list to lower your quarterly numbers, see our complete gig worker deduction guide. For the bigger framework on SE tax specifically, our SE tax explainer covers the 15.3% breakdown.

When Cash Flow Does Not Match the Due Date

Quarterly dates do not care about your bank balance. If a payment date arrives and you do not have the cash because last week was slow, you have options:

  • Pay as much as you can on time and add the rest with the next paycheck (still reduces penalty)
  • Set up an IRS payment plan after you file (penalties continue but smaller)
  • Bridge with a cash advance to pay on time and protect your safe harbor

A PennyLime advance can cover a $1,500 quarterly payment same day if your earnings are uneven, with weekly ACH repayment from your normal deposit account.

Bottom Line

Mark April 15, June 15, September 15, and January 15. Save 25 to 30% of every gig payment. Pay through Direct Pay or EFTPS. Track confirmations. That is the whole system.

If a quarter sneaks up and your bank balance is short, apply for a PennyLime cash advance and stay on schedule with the IRS.

This is general tax information, not advice. Consult a CPA for your situation. PennyLime is a service of 770 Technology LLC.

title: Self-Employment Tax Explained for New Gig Workers
metaTitle: Self-Employment Tax Explained: New Gig Worker Guide
metaDescription: What self-employment tax is, how the 15.3% rate works, and how Uber, DoorDash, and Instacart drivers calculate and reduce it. Plain English guide.
excerpt: Your first year of gig work, you discover a tax your W-2 friends never paid. Here is what self-employment tax is, why it exists, and how to keep your share manageable.
body:
The first time a new gig worker files taxes, the surprise usually has nothing to do with income tax. It has to do with a 15.3% line called self-employment tax. If you came from a W-2 job, you never saw this number, because your employer paid half and quietly deducted the other half from your paycheck. As a 1099 contractor, both halves are on you.

This article explains exactly what SE tax is, who owes it, how to calculate it, and the legal ways to reduce it.

What Self-Employment Tax Actually Is

SE tax funds Social Security and Medicare for self-employed people. It is the equivalent of the FICA taxes that get withheld from W-2 paychecks. The split:

  • 12.4% Social Security (on income up to the annual cap, $168,600 for 2024 income, indexed up for 2025)
  • 2.9% Medicare (no cap)
  • Total base rate: 15.3%

Additional Medicare tax of 0.9% kicks in on self-employment income above $200,000 single or $250,000 married filing jointly.

Why Gig Workers Owe Both Halves

For a W-2 employee, the employer pays 7.65% and the employee pays 7.65% (withheld from each paycheck). The IRS sees a 1099 contractor as their own employer and their own employee. You pay both halves.

If you previously earned $50,000 at a W-2 job, you saw $3,825 of FICA come out of your paychecks. Your employer paid another $3,825 you never noticed. As a gig worker on the same $50,000 net, you owe the full $7,650.

How to Calculate SE Tax (The Real Formula)

Three steps:

Step 1: Calculate Net Self-Employment Earnings

Gross gig income minus business expenses. From your Schedule C, this is Line 31 (net profit).

Example: $50,000 gross from Uber and DoorDash, $18,000 in expenses (mostly mileage), net $32,000.

Step 2: Multiply by 92.35%

This is the "employer-equivalent" adjustment. The IRS lets you reduce your SE earnings by 7.65% before applying the tax, mirroring how a W-2 employer's share would be deducted before FICA.

$32,000 times 0.9235 equals $29,552.

Step 3: Multiply by 15.3%

$29,552 times 0.153 equals $4,521 of SE tax.

That number goes on Schedule SE and flows to Schedule 2 Line 4 of your 1040.

The Half-Deduction You Get Back

The IRS lets you deduct half of your SE tax as an adjustment to income on Schedule 1. This is automatic and lowers your income tax (not your SE tax).

In the example above, $4,521 SE tax means a $2,260 deduction on Schedule 1 Line 15. If you are in the 12% bracket, that saves you another $271 of income tax.

When SE Tax Kicks In

If your net self-employment earnings (after the 92.35% adjustment) are less than $400 for the year, you do not owe SE tax. Most gig workers cross that threshold within the first week.

How SE Tax Stacks With Income Tax

SE tax and income tax are separate calculations layered on the same income. Here is how a $32,000 net Schedule C looks for a single filer in 2025:

ItemAmount
Net Schedule C income$32,000
SE tax (15.3% on 92.35%)$4,521
Half SE tax deduction($2,260)
Adjusted gross income$29,740
Standard deduction (2025 single)($15,000)
QBI deduction (20% of qualified business income, simplified)~($2,948)
Taxable income~$11,792
Federal income tax (10-12% brackets)~$1,260
Plus SE tax$4,521
Total federal tax$5,781

That is roughly 18% of net income, with SE tax doing most of the work. State tax sits on top.

Why the Standard Deduction Does Not Help SE Tax

This is the part that trips up new gig workers. Your standard deduction (or itemized deductions) reduces taxable income for income tax purposes. It does NOT reduce SE tax. SE tax is calculated on net Schedule C earnings, before the standard deduction even enters the picture.

The only way to lower SE tax is to lower your Schedule C net profit. That means more legitimate business expenses (mileage, phone, supplies) or fewer gross receipts.

Legal Ways to Reduce SE Tax

1. Track Every Deductible Expense

Every dollar of expense you claim is a dollar that does not pay 15.3% SE tax. Our complete deduction list covers the biggest categories.

2. Max Out the Mileage Deduction

For most gig drivers, mileage is the largest single deduction. At 70 cents/mile for 2025, 15,000 business miles is a $10,500 deduction (saving roughly $1,600 in SE tax alone). See our mileage app roundup if you are not tracking yet.

3. Contribute to a SEP-IRA or Solo 401(k)

Retirement contributions reduce income tax but NOT SE tax. Still worth doing for the income tax savings and long-term wealth, but do not expect SE tax relief.

4. Elect S-Corp Status (Past a Certain Income)

If your net Schedule C income clears roughly $60,000 to $80,000, an S-corp election (via an LLC or corporation) can split your income into a salary (subject to payroll tax) and distributions (not subject to SE tax). See our LLC vs sole prop article for the math.

5. Hire Your Spouse or Children (Legitimately)

If they actually work for the business, their wages are an expense. Children under 18 in a sole proprietorship are not subject to FICA on those wages. Tread carefully and document everything.

What SE Tax Pays For

SE tax is not punishment. It buys you future Social Security and Medicare benefits. Every quarter you pay builds your earnings record.

  • Social Security: 40 credits (10 years of earning at the threshold) qualifies you for retirement benefits
  • Medicare: same credit system, qualifies you for Medicare at 65
  • Disability and survivor benefits: also based on credits

If you completely skip SE tax (illegal anyway), you also skip building your retirement and disability foundation.

How to Pay SE Tax Throughout the Year

SE tax is part of your quarterly estimated tax payments. You do not pay it as a separate line item; it is bundled into the same quarterly amount you send to the IRS.

Most gig workers save 25 to 30% of each platform payment in a separate account specifically for quarterly tax payments. That bucket covers both SE tax and federal income tax (plus state if applicable).

Common Misconceptions

  • "Forming an LLC eliminates SE tax." No. A single-member LLC defaults to sole prop tax treatment. Only an S-corp election changes the SE tax calculation.
  • "Personal deductions like mortgage interest reduce SE tax." No. Only business expenses reduce SE tax.
  • "Health insurance premiums reduce SE tax." No. The self-employed health insurance deduction lowers income tax, not SE tax.
  • "Retirement contributions reduce SE tax." No. They lower income tax only.

Where SE Tax Shows on Your Tax Return

  • Calculated on Schedule SE
  • Half-deduction on Schedule 1 Line 15
  • Total flows to Schedule 2 Line 4
  • Schedule 2 total goes to Form 1040 Line 23

SE Tax for Workers With Both W-2 and 1099 Income

If you drive Uber on weekends and hold a W-2 job during the week, you have to handle both. Your W-2 wages have FICA withheld automatically. Your 1099 earnings owe full SE tax.

The Social Security portion of SE tax has an annual cap (the Social Security wage base, around $168,600 for 2024 and indexed up each year). If your W-2 wages already maxed out the cap, your 1099 earnings only owe the 2.9% Medicare portion of SE tax, not the full 15.3%. Schedule SE handles this calculation automatically.

This is why high earners with side gigs often face a smaller SE tax hit than they expected.

Quick SE Tax Reference Table

Net Schedule CApproximate SE Tax
$10,000$1,413
$25,000$3,532
$50,000$7,065
$75,000$10,597
$100,000$14,130

Use this as a budgeting reference. Set aside at least the SE tax amount in your tax savings account, then add federal and state income tax on top.

What About Withholding From Your Spouse's W-2

If you are married filing jointly and your spouse has a W-2 job, you can increase their withholding to cover your SE tax. Submit a new W-4 with an additional withholding amount, and the IRS treats withholding as paid evenly throughout the year (unlike estimated payments, which are credited on the date paid).

This is the easiest way to avoid quarterly estimated payments. Your spouse's payroll covers both their income tax and your SE plus income tax through a single extra withholding amount.

Run the numbers: figure out your projected joint federal tax liability, subtract current withholding plus expected estimates, and divide the gap by the number of remaining pay periods. That dollar amount goes on the new W-4.

When SE Tax Bites the Hardest

Your first full year of gig work, with no prior-year estimated payments and a healthy net income, is the worst surprise. You can face a $5,000+ tax bill in April with no withholding to cushion it.

If you find yourself in that spot, breathe. The IRS will set up a payment plan. And a short-term PennyLime cash advance can cover the bill and protect your filing status while you smooth out cash flow with weekly ACH repayment.

Bottom Line

SE tax is 15.3% on 92.35% of your net Schedule C income. Half is deductible on your income tax return. It funds your future Social Security and Medicare. The only way to reduce it is to legitimately lower your net business income (deductions) or restructure (S-corp at higher incomes).

Set aside 25 to 30% of every gig payment. Pay quarterly. Track your miles and expenses. If a tax surprise hits and you need bridge funding, apply for a PennyLime cash advance in minutes.

This is general tax information, not advice. Consult a CPA for your situation. PennyLime is a service of 770 Technology LLC.

title: Home Office Deduction for Gig Workers: Can You Claim It
metaTitle: Home Office Deduction for Gig Workers: Who Qualifies
metaDescription: Most gig drivers do not qualify for the home office deduction. Here is who actually can claim it, the regular vs simplified methods, and the audit risk.
excerpt: The home office deduction sounds great until you read the rules. For most rideshare and delivery drivers, the answer is no. For some gig workers, it is a real write-off.
body:
The home office deduction is one of the most asked-about and most misunderstood write-offs for gig workers. Social media is full of "deduct your home office!" posts that skip the part where the IRS has very specific rules and gig drivers usually do not meet them.

This article explains the two tests you have to pass, who actually qualifies, the two calculation methods, and the audit risk so you can make an honest decision.

The Two Tests You Have to Pass

To deduct any home office expense, your space must be used:

  • Regularly for business. Occasional use does not count.
  • Exclusively for business. The space must be used ONLY for business, with rare exceptions.

Both tests have to be met. "Regularly" is usually easy. "Exclusively" is where most gig workers fail.

What "Exclusive Use" Means

The IRS wants a clearly identifiable area used only for your business. A spare bedroom converted to an office, a sectioned-off corner of a basement, a dedicated desk with no personal computer use. Not:

  • The kitchen table where you do bookkeeping and also eat dinner
  • A couch corner where you sometimes answer emails
  • A guest room that occasionally hosts guests
  • Your bed where you scroll the app at night

If your kids ever do homework at the desk, it fails. If you watch Netflix in the chair on weekends, it fails. The bar is strict.

What "Regular Use" Means

Used on a continuing basis for business. Once a quarter does not count. A few hours a week, every week, generally does.

The Principal Place of Business Test

On top of regular and exclusive use, your home office has to meet one of these:

  • It is your principal place of business (where you do the main work), OR
  • You meet clients there, OR
  • It is a separate structure used only for business

This is where most gig drivers fail decisively. Your principal place of business is your car. The car is where you actually earn revenue. A home office for administrative work might still qualify if:

  • You use it regularly and exclusively for administrative activities (bookkeeping, scheduling, customer communication)
  • You have no other fixed location where you do those administrative tasks

For most rideshare and delivery drivers, this is a stretch. You handle administrative tasks in the car between trips, on your phone, or at the kitchen table. Claiming a home office is hard to defend.

Gig Workers Who Might Actually Qualify

  • Freelance designers, developers, writers. The home office IS the workplace. Strongest case.
  • Online tutors and coaches. Sessions happen in the dedicated space. Strong case.
  • Etsy sellers. If you have a dedicated craft area used only for inventory, packing, and listing work. Reasonable case.
  • Photographers with a home editing studio. If exclusively used. Reasonable case.
  • Bookkeepers, consultants, virtual assistants. Home is the office.

Gig Workers Who Usually Do NOT Qualify

  • Uber, Lyft, and other rideshare drivers
  • DoorDash, Uber Eats, Grubhub, Instacart, and other delivery couriers
  • TaskRabbit and Rover providers who work at client locations
  • Anyone whose actual revenue work happens in a car or at a customer site

If 80% of your time on the clock is in the car or out of the home, the home office is at best a small administrative space that the IRS will scrutinize.

If You Qualify: The Two Calculation Methods

Simplified Method

$5 per square foot of qualifying home office space, up to 300 square feet. Maximum deduction: $1,500.

Example: 150 square feet dedicated home office equals $750 deduction.

What is good about it:

  • No depreciation recapture when you sell the home
  • No tracking of utilities, mortgage interest, or repairs
  • One line on Schedule C

What is bad about it:

  • Capped at $1,500
  • Cannot deduct home office expenses that exceed the $5/sqft

Regular Method

Calculate actual home expenses (rent or mortgage interest, utilities, insurance, repairs, depreciation) and multiply by the business use percentage (office square footage divided by total home square footage).

Example: 150 sqft office in a 1,500 sqft home equals 10% business use. Annual home expenses of $24,000 (rent, utilities, insurance) times 10% equals $2,400 deduction. Plus depreciation if you own.

What is good about it:

  • No cap
  • Captures the real cost
  • Includes a depreciation share if you own

What is bad about it:

  • Heavy recordkeeping
  • Depreciation recapture when you sell (you pay back the depreciation as ordinary income)
  • More audit attention

Side-by-Side Comparison

FactorSimplifiedRegular
Max deduction$1,500Unlimited (proportional)
RecordkeepingJust sqftAll home expenses
DepreciationNot allowedRequired
Recapture on saleNoneYes (on depreciation)
Audit riskLowerHigher

Items You Can Always Deduct (No Home Office Required)

Even if you do not claim a home office, you can still deduct:

  • Cell phone (business use percentage)
  • Internet (business use percentage, if used for business)
  • Office supplies used for the business
  • A laptop or printer used for the business

These are separate Schedule C line items and do not require passing the home office tests.

The Audit Risk Reality

The home office deduction historically attracted more audit attention than other Schedule C items because the IRS knows it gets abused. The simplified method reduced audit interest somewhat, but a large regular-method home office on a return with low income still draws scrutiny.

If you claim a home office:

  • Take photos of the space
  • Keep a floor plan with measurements
  • Make sure the space is truly exclusive (no personal items)
  • Be ready to explain how it is your principal place of business or used exclusively for administrative work

Where the Deduction Goes on Your Tax Return

If you use the simplified method, the deduction appears on Schedule C Line 30 (no separate form needed).

If you use the regular method, you file Form 8829 (Expenses for Business Use of Your Home) and carry the total to Schedule C Line 30.

If You Are a Gig Driver Asking Anyway

Two honest answers:

If you have a dedicated room or corner used regularly and exclusively for administrative work (bookkeeping, customer service, scheduling), and you have no other fixed location for that work, you might qualify for a small simplified-method deduction. Be conservative. A small $250 to $500 deduction for legitimate administrative space is defensible. A $1,500 max from a kitchen table is not.

If the answer is "sort of" or "I do my texting on the couch," skip it. The deduction is small, the audit risk is real, and your bigger wins are mileage, phone, and supplies. See our full deduction list for what to focus on instead.

Other Misconceptions

  • "I can deduct my whole rent." No. Only the business-use percentage.
  • "I can deduct repairs to the rest of the house." No. Only repairs to the home office itself, or the business-use share of whole-home repairs.
  • "I can deduct improvements like a new kitchen." No. Personal-use improvements are not deductible.
  • "Renters cannot claim it." False. Renters can deduct the business-use percentage of rent.

What This Means for Your Overall Tax Plan

For most gig drivers, the home office is not where your savings live. Mileage, phone, supplies, and platform fees are where the real money is. If you are a freelancer or remote worker with a real dedicated office, the simplified method is usually the right tool because the recordkeeping is minimal and the audit risk is low.

Pair the home office with your quarterly estimated tax plan and make sure your overall withholding plus estimates covers your liability.

Special Case: Storage of Inventory or Samples

The IRS makes an exception to the "exclusive use" rule for storage of inventory or product samples. If you sell on Etsy and store inventory in a garage corner, that corner can count as a home office even if you sometimes walk through it for other reasons. The space must be a separately identifiable area, and selling product must be your trade or business.

This does not help rideshare drivers (no inventory) but does help Etsy sellers, Amazon FBA preppers, and gig workers who do hands-on product fulfillment.

Daycare Exception

If you run a daycare from your home, the exclusive use rule is also relaxed. Most gig workers will not benefit, but if you also operate a licensed family childcare from your home alongside gig driving, the daycare portion has its own friendly rules under Form 8829.

When Tax Season Cash Flow Gets Tight

Whether or not you claim a home office, a surprise tax bill or a slow earnings week can squeeze your budget. Bottom Line

Most gig drivers do not qualify for the home office deduction. The car is the workplace. If you do legitimately have an exclusive, regularly-used home administrative space, use the simplified method and take a small honest deduction. If you do not, skip it and focus on mileage and phone, which are bigger and easier to defend.

title: Vehicle Depreciation on Taxes: How Gig Drivers Handle It
metaTitle: Vehicle Depreciation for Gig Drivers: Tax Guide 2026
metaDescription: How vehicle depreciation works for Uber, DoorDash, and Instacart drivers in 2026. Section 179, bonus depreciation, MACRS, and the standard mileage rate.
excerpt: If you bought a car for gig work, depreciation is one of the largest write-offs you will ever take. Here is how the rules work and when you should use them.
body:
Depreciation is the IRS-approved way to deduct the cost of a long-lived asset over time. For gig drivers, the asset is the car, and the rules are some of the most confusing in the tax code. Section 179, bonus depreciation, MACRS, luxury auto limits, business-use thresholds. They all matter, and they interact.

This article unpacks how depreciation actually works for rideshare drivers and couriers, when to use it, when to skip it for the standard mileage rate, and what happens if you sell the car later.

What Depreciation Means

You bought a car for $30,000. You did not get to deduct $30,000 the year you bought it (in most cases). Instead, the IRS lets you deduct a portion each year over the asset's "useful life," which for vehicles is five years under MACRS (Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System).

If you use 80% of the car for business, only 80% of the cost is depreciable. The other 20% is personal and never deductible.

Standard Mileage Versus Actual Expense

Depreciation is only relevant if you use the actual expense method. If you use the standard mileage rate (70 cents per mile for 2025 returns), depreciation is baked into the rate. You do not separately deduct it.

For most gig drivers, standard mileage beats actual expense, which means depreciation is not a separate line item. See our mileage versus actual expense comparison for the math.

If you are using actual expense, depreciation is where the real money is, and the rest of this article matters.

MACRS: The Default Schedule

Under the Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System, passenger vehicles depreciate over five years on a half-year convention. The percentages are:

YearDepreciation %
120%
232%
319.2%
411.52%
511.52%
65.76%

Wait, that is six years. The half-year convention means you only get half a year of depreciation in year one, so the schedule extends into a sixth tax year.

Luxury Auto Caps

The IRS limits how much you can depreciate on a "luxury auto," which for tax purposes means almost any passenger car. For vehicles placed in service in 2024 (the latest published year as of 2025), the caps were:

  • Year 1: $12,400 (or $20,400 with bonus depreciation)
  • Year 2: $19,800
  • Year 3: $11,900
  • Year 4 and beyond: $7,160 until fully depreciated

2025 and 2026 caps adjust slightly for inflation. Check the latest IRS Rev. Proc. when you file.

These caps apply to passenger cars and light trucks and SUVs under 6,000 lbs GVWR.

The 6,000 Pound Rule

If your vehicle has a Gross Vehicle Weight Rating over 6,000 lbs, the luxury auto caps do not apply. Many full-size SUVs (Suburban, Tahoe, Yukon, Expedition, Sequoia) and pickup trucks qualify.

For these vehicles, you can use Section 179 expensing and bonus depreciation more aggressively. Section 179 allows up to $30,500 in immediate expensing for heavy SUVs (2024 limit, adjusts for inflation), and bonus depreciation covers a percentage of the remaining cost.

Bonus Depreciation Schedule

Year Placed in ServiceBonus %
2022 and earlier100%
202380%
202460%
202540%
202620%
20270%

Bonus depreciation is phasing out unless Congress extends it. For a heavy SUV bought in 2025, you can take 40% bonus depreciation on the business-use portion of the cost above any Section 179 limit, then MACRS on the rest.

Worked Example: $40,000 Heavy SUV, 80% Business Use

You bought a 6,500 lb GVWR SUV in 2025 for $40,000. You use it 80% for DoorDash and Uber Eats. Business basis: $40,000 times 0.80 equals $32,000.

  • Section 179 expensing (up to heavy SUV cap, $30,500 in 2024 with inflation adjustment): roughly $30,500
  • Remaining basis: $32,000 minus $30,500 equals $1,500
  • Bonus depreciation at 40% (2025): $600
  • Remaining for MACRS: $900
  • Year 1 MACRS at 20%: $180
  • Total Year 1 depreciation: roughly $31,280

That is a massive Year 1 deduction. The catch: you cannot use Section 179 to create a loss, and you must maintain over 50% business use or face recapture.

Worked Example: $30,000 Passenger Car, 70% Business Use

You bought a 2025 Toyota Camry for $30,000. You use it 70% for Uber. Business basis: $30,000 times 0.70 equals $21,000.

Under MACRS only, Year 1 at 20% would be $4,200. But the luxury auto cap applies. Year 1 cap (without bonus): $12,400, prorated to business use ($12,400 times 0.70 equals $8,680).

$4,200 is below the cap, so you deduct $4,200. With bonus depreciation, you could potentially deduct more in Year 1, up to the bonus-included cap. The luxury auto rules are intricate; this is where a CPA earns their fee.

Standard Mileage Beats Most Actual Expense Math

For a $30,000 Camry driven 18,000 business miles:

  • Standard mileage: 18,000 times $0.70 equals $12,600
  • Actual expense with depreciation: roughly $9,000 to $11,000 depending on details

Standard mileage usually wins for typical gig vehicles. Depreciation shines for heavy vehicles, expensive vehicles, or first-year aggressive expensing on a 6,000-plus lb SUV.

The Business Use Percentage Cliff

Section 179 and bonus depreciation require over 50% business use. If your business use drops below 50% in any year within the recovery period, you have to recapture the excess depreciation as ordinary income.

That is a brutal surprise. If you took $30,000 of Section 179 in year one and dropped to 40% business use in year three, you owe back the depreciation that exceeded straight-line at 40%.

Track your miles carefully. Use one of the apps in our mileage app roundup.

What Happens When You Sell

Depreciation recapture is the other ugly word. When you sell a depreciated vehicle, gain up to the amount of accumulated depreciation is taxed as ordinary income (not capital gain).

Example: bought a vehicle for $40,000, depreciated $25,000 over five years, sold for $20,000. Your adjusted basis is $15,000. You realized a $5,000 gain ($20,000 sale minus $15,000 basis). The entire $5,000 is recaptured as ordinary income.

Personal-use proportions complicate this. If 70% of the vehicle was business, only 70% of the sale and the basis enter the calculation.

Standard Mileage and Hidden Depreciation

If you use the standard mileage rate, a portion of the rate represents depreciation. The IRS publishes the per-mile depreciation amount each year (it was 30 cents for 2024, slightly less for 2025). You must reduce your basis by accumulated standard mileage depreciation when you sell the vehicle.

That means even standard mileage users face a recapture issue at sale time. It is just usually smaller than a heavy Section 179 user faces.

Recordkeeping You Need

  • Date placed in service (first day you drove for a platform)
  • Purchase price and acquisition documents
  • Business use percentage each year (mileage log)
  • Method elected (Section 179, bonus, MACRS, or standard mileage)
  • Form 4562 each year you take depreciation

Form 4562 and Where Depreciation Goes

Form 4562 is where you compute depreciation. The total flows to Schedule C Line 13. Vehicle information also appears on Schedule C Part IV.

Leased Vehicles and Depreciation

If you lease instead of buy, you do not depreciate the vehicle (the leasing company does). Instead, you deduct the business-use portion of lease payments under actual expense, subject to a "lease inclusion amount" for vehicles above an IRS-set fair market value threshold.

The lease inclusion amount is published by the IRS each year and effectively reduces your deduction by a small amount to align lease and purchase treatment. Most reasonably-priced lease deals have a modest inclusion amount. Exotic and luxury vehicle leases face larger reductions.

Leased vehicles also lock in your method choice for the entire lease, so if you elect standard mileage on a lease in year one, you must use standard mileage through the lease's end.

Common Mistakes

  • Mixing methods. If you elected standard mileage in year one, you cannot just switch to Section 179 in year three.
  • Section 179 on a personal vehicle. You need over 50% business use, and the IRS may push back on aggressive claims.
  • Forgetting recapture. Selling the depreciated vehicle is a taxable event.
  • Claiming bonus on a vehicle bought before placement in service. The clock starts when you first use it for business.

Should You Use Depreciation or Standard Mileage

Standard mileage if:

  • You drive a typical passenger car (under 6,000 lbs)
  • You drive a lot of business miles
  • You want simpler recordkeeping
  • This is year one for the vehicle

Actual expense with depreciation if:

  • You bought a heavy SUV or pickup (over 6,000 lbs)
  • Your business use percentage is high (over 80%)
  • You want a large Year 1 deduction (and accept the recapture risk later)
  • Your fuel and repair costs are unusually high

Cash Flow Around the Vehicle Decision

Buying a vehicle for gig work can mean a 5-figure down payment or a major loan. If your earnings are uneven and you want to time the purchase for a high-deduction year, a short-term PennyLime cash advance can bridge the gap between when you need the cash and when your platform deposits land. $500 to $10,000, no credit check, funded in 24 to 48 hours.

Bottom Line

Most gig drivers should stick with standard mileage and never touch Section 179 or bonus depreciation. The math is simpler and usually larger. If you bought a heavy SUV or truck, the actual expense method with aggressive depreciation can produce a bigger Year 1 deduction, but you take on recapture risk and tighter recordkeeping.
